source.title: Computational Design of Enantiocomplementary Epoxide Hydrolases for Asymmetric Synthesis of Aliphatic and Aromatic Diols

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study aims to computationally design enantiocomplementary epoxide hydrolases for asymmetric synthesis of aliphatic and aromatic diols from meso-epoxides. The challenge is to control regioselectivity of water attack to produce either (R,R)- or (S,S)-diols. Prior directed evolution required extensive screening; here, a computational framework (CASCO) combining Rosetta design and high-throughput MD simulations is used to reduce library size and predict enantioselectivity. The work explores the reliability and limitations of this approach across three substrates of varying size and aromaticity.

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Introduces biocatalysis for fine chemicals, need for enzyme engineering, and limitations of directed evolution (high-throughput screening required). Proposes computational design as an alternative, describing the CASCO framework: Rosetta for primary design, then high-throughput MD simulations to score near-attack conformations (NACs) for ranking. Previous work on LEH showed CASCO variants matched best from ~4700 screened. This study further explores computational redesign of LEH for three meso-epoxides to assess prediction reliability.
process_signals: Directed evolution is successful but requires high-throughput screening, which is time-consuming and expensive for enantioselectivity.
Computational methods can explore multiple mutations simultaneously, allowing larger jumps in sequence space.
CASCO framework previously tested on LEH, showing performance similar to best from large directed evolution library.
Goal: further explore possibilities and limitations of computational redesign for enantioselective LEH variants.
methods: CASCO framework: Rosetta scoring and search algorithm for primary design, then high-throughput MD simulations (HTMI-MD) with NAC scoring for ranking.
NAC criteria: hydrogen bond between leaving oxygen and D101, close distance between nucleophilic water and attacked oxirane carbon, near-linear orientation.
MD uses 20–80 short simulations (10 ps) with independent initial velocities for better conformational sampling.
metrics: Frequency of reactive poses (NAC%) during MD
failures_or_limits: Reliability of computational predictions is a serious challenge.
Directed evolution still requires substantial experimental screening.

section_gists.heading: Computational design
section_gists.gist: Describes design workflow: substrates docked with restraints for reactive configuration (NAC). Rosetta Monte Carlo optimized 11 active-site positions (randomized to 9 hydrophobic residues) for proRR or proSS attack. Generated thousands of designs per substrate. MD screening: 15,000 Rosetta designs subjected to at least 5×10 ps MD; top designs received up to 80×10 ps and 5×100 ps. NAC% scored on-the-fly. Selection criteria: high NAC% for desired enantiomer, low for opposite. Top 5 designs per enantiomer per substrate chosen for experimental testing.
process_signals: Design targeted 11 positions with 9 hydrophobic residues each, generating thousands of variants.
MD screening used multiple short simulations for better sampling.
Selection based on NAC%: high for desired enantiomer, low for opposite.
Top 5 designs per enantiomer per substrate selected for lab testing.
methods: Rosetta design with restraints for NAC (H-bond to D101, distance and angle constraints).
Monte Carlo optimization of side-chain identities and geometries.
HTMI-MD: 5–80×10 ps and 5×100 ps simulations, NAC scoring on-the-fly.
Substrates: cyclopentene oxide (1a), butene oxide (2a), stilbene oxide (3a).
failures_or_limits: Not all Rosetta designs passed MD screening; only top ranked were selected.

section_gists.heading: Experimental characterization
section_gists.gist: 28 new LEH variants constructed in thermostable template LEH-P. 26 active on target substrate. 21/26 (77%) showed predicted enantioselectivity (10% ee cutoff). For cyclopentene oxide (1a): 9/10 active, all predicted correctly; proSS designs gave higher ee (56–85%) than proRR. For butene oxide (2a): 10/10 active, 8/10 predicted correctly; proSS better (best 77% ee). For stilbene oxide (3a): 6/8 active predicted correctly; two mismatches (52A predicted (R,R) but gave (S,S) with 97% ee). MD corrected two Rosetta designs (51A, 60A) from proSS to proRR. Highly enantioselective variants obtained: >91% ee for both enantiomers of 3a. Thermostability maintained (avg Tm 62.7°C vs 70°C template).
process_signals: 28 variants tested; 26 active.
77% prediction accuracy overall.
ProSS designs generally outperformed proRR for small substrates.
MD corrected Rosetta predictions for two stilbene oxide variants.
Two prediction failures for stilbene oxide, including one with high ee opposite to prediction.
High enantioselectivities achieved for stilbene oxide (up to >99% ee).
methods: Site-directed mutagenesis (QuikChange) in thermostable LEH-P template.
Expression in E. coli, His-tag purification.
Activity assays: mixing enzyme with epoxide, measuring diol formation.
Chiral analysis of diols.
Thermostability measured by apparent Tm.
failures_or_limits: Two mismatches for stilbene oxide: 52A (predicted (R,R), experimental (S,S) 97% ee) and another unspecified.
Some designs had low activity (<1% wild-type).
ProRR designs for small substrates gave lower ee than proSS.

section_gists.heading: Preparative scale conversions
section_gists.gist: Tested variants 60A (proRR) and 41B (proSS) for stilbene oxide at 50 mM substrate. Best conditions: 10–15% dioxane, 40°C. 60A gave 98% conversion, >99% ee (R,R). 41B gave 63% conversion, 88% ee (S,S). THF and ethyl acetate biphasic system gave lower conversions. Demonstrates practical applicability.
process_signals: Preparative scale validated for best variants.
Cosolvent screening: dioxane best, THF worse, ethyl acetate biphasic failed.
High conversions and ee achieved, especially for proRR variant.
methods: Reactions at 1 mL scale, 50 mM substrate, 320 μM enzyme, 50 mM HEPES pH 8.0.
Cosolvents: 1,4-dioxane, THF, ethyl acetate.
Temperatures: 30°C and 40°C.
Chiral HPLC analysis.
failures_or_limits: ProSS variant 41B gave lower conversion (63%) and ee (88%) than proRR variant.
Ethyl acetate biphasic system gave no conversion for 60A.

section_gists.heading: Structural origin of enantioselectivity
section_gists.gist: Enantioselectivity determined by substrate positioning relative to fixed nucleophilic water. ProSS selectivity: substrate shifted toward central side (near β4, β5, β6, H4) due to steric hindrance on peripheral side (e.g., L35W, I116V). ProRR selectivity: substrate shifted toward peripheral side due to steric hindrance on central side (e.g., L114W, I116F). For stilbene oxide, steric effects alone insufficient; π-π interactions may play a role (e.g., I116F giving proSS selectivity unexpectedly). Substrate rotation not observed.
process_signals: Water position fixed; selectivity from substrate sliding.
For small substrates, steric hindrance explains selectivity.
For stilbene oxide, mutations like I116F gave opposite selectivity to steric expectation, suggesting π-π interactions.
No substrate rotation observed in models or MD.
methods: Inspection of Rosetta designed structures and average HTML-MD structures.
Comparison of substrate positions in proSS and proRR designs.
failures_or_limits: Steric effects alone cannot explain stilbene oxide selectivity; need to consider π-π interactions.
Combined effects of mutations difficult to rationalize.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: CASCO reduced library size: only 5 variants per enantiomer per substrate tested, yielding high enantioselectivities. Prediction accuracy qualitative (77% correct stereopreference) but not quantitative (NAC% not correlated with activity). Best results with stilbene oxide due to more steric and π-π interaction opportunities. Lower activity of designs mainly due to increased KM, not decreased kcat, unlike directed evolution variants. Rosetta design failures for stilbene oxide attributed to inability to model π-π interactions; MD corrected some. Need improved scoring for aromatic interactions.
process_signals: CASCO effectively enriched libraries, reducing screening burden.
Qualitative prediction of stereopreference achieved; quantitative activity prediction not possible.
Stilbene oxide gave highest enantioselectivities due to bulk and aromatic interactions.
Activity loss from increased KM, not kcat, which is acceptable for preparative use.
Rosetta design errors for stilbene oxide due to missing π-π interactions; MD partially corrected.
Future work: improve scoring for aromatic interactions.
methods: Comparison with directed evolution results.
Kinetic analysis (KM, kcat) to explain activity changes.
failures_or_limits: Rosetta design failures for aromatic substrates due to π-π interactions.
MD screening still gave two mismatches for stilbene oxide.
Need for better scoring functions for aromatic interactions.

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: Computational design and MD simulations can predict enantioselectivity for small aliphatic substrates. Highly selective variants for aromatic diols obtained, but prediction accuracy lower. Need rapid scoring methods that include π-π interactions for aromatic substrates.
process_signals: Success for aliphatic substrates.
Partial success for aromatic substrates; prediction less accurate.
Limitation: current scoring insufficient for π-π interactions.
failures_or_limits: Lower prediction accuracy for aromatic substrates.
Need improved scoring for π-π interactions.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The CASCO framework can design enantiocomplementary LEH variants with reduced experimental screening.
MD-based NAC scoring can predict stereopreference qualitatively.
Steric hindrance is the main determinant of enantioselectivity for small substrates, but aromatic interactions become important for larger substrates.
Computational design may fail when interactions like π-π stacking are not accurately modeled.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Can the prediction accuracy for aromatic substrates be improved by incorporating π-π interaction scoring?
Why did variant 52A show opposite enantioselectivity with high ee?
Can the CASCO protocol be generalized to other enzyme systems and substrates?
What is the limit of substrate size and complexity for reliable computational design?

events.summary: Computational design can reduce library size and predict enantioselectivity, allowing larger jumps in sequence space.
Design targeted 11 active-site positions randomized to 9 hydrophobic residues for proRR or proSS attack.
MD screening performed on 15,000 Rosetta designs with at least 5×10 ps simulations; top designs received up to 80×10 ps and 5×100 ps.
Reliability of computational predictions is a serious challenge.
Obtaining unique binding orientations (high enantioselectivity) is easier with bulkier substrates.

events.summary: Stilbene oxide designs had higher NAC frequencies and predicted enantioselectivities than smaller substrates.
Visual inspection of top-ranked designs revealed structural problems, especially for stilbene oxide.
numbers_or_metrics: 10 of 20 inspected variants for stilbene oxide displayed structural problems

events.summary: 77% of active variants showed predicted enantioselectivity (10% ee cutoff).

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains experimental validation, MD screening results, and kinetic analysis.

events.summary: Kinetic analysis reveals that lower catalytic activities of most designs are due to higher KM, not lower kcat.
numbers_or_metrics: 76-fold increase in KM for 1a in mutant 46C

events.summary: For stilbene oxide designs, kcat values were lower than wild-type, but KM values were better, indicating that MD simulations do not predict reactivities across substrates.
High enantioselectivities for stilbene oxide are attributed to restricted conformational freedom and better KM due to increased active site volume.
numbers_or_metrics: ca. 67 Å³ additional space in mutant 60A

events.summary: Preparative scale conversions of stilbene oxide using variants 60A and 41B were tested under various conditions, achieving up to 98% conversion and >99% ee for (R,R)-diol.
Structural analysis shows enantioselectivity is determined by substrate positioning relative to a fixed nucleophilic water, with steric hindrance steering selectivity for small substrates.
For stilbene oxide, steric effects alone cannot explain selectivity; π-π interactions may play a role, as mutation I116F unexpectedly gives (S,S)-selectivity.
MD-based NAC scoring provides qualitative but not quantitative prediction of enantioselectivity; no correlation between NAC% and activity.
MD simulations do not account for energy barriers along reaction coordinates, limiting quantitative reactivity prediction across substrates.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains kinetic analysis, preparative scale experiments, structural analysis of enantioselectivity, and discussion of prediction accuracy and limitations.

events.summary: Best enantioselectivities obtained with stilbene oxide 3a, with >99% ee for (R,R)-diol and 97% ee for (S,S)-diol.
Four enantioselectivities with stilbene oxide opposite to Rosetta design target; two corrected by MD.
Computational design and MD simulations predict enantioselectivity well for small aliphatic substrates but less accurately for aromatic substrates.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains analysis of enantioselectivity results, explanation of activity loss, failure of Rosetta design for aromatic substrate, and conclusions about prediction accuracy and limitations.

events.summary: Preparative scale conversion of stilbene oxide using designed variants 60A and 41B.
Author contributions: H.J.W. designed mutants; H.A. constructed, isolated, and characterized mutants; catalytic activities measured by H.A., P.J., D.I.C.; preparative experiments by D.I.C. and M.T.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains only experimental methods for preparative scale reactions and author contributions, with no new research evolution events beyond what was already described in earlier sections.

evidence_bank.quote: Multiple mutations can be explored simultaneously, allowing for larger jumps in sequence space than directed evolution and structure-based rational mutagenesis.

in silico screening of enzyme variants by docking and high-throughput molecular dynamics simulations makes it possible to predict enzyme properties and decrease library size for experimental evaluation from thousands to dozens.
The explored sequence space was created by targeting 11 selected positions around the active site (Figure 1B) with randomization to any of the nine hydrophobic residues (AFGILMPVW).

For each of the 15000 Rosetta designs, at least five independently initialized MD runs of 10 ps were performed (Table 1). Designs that passed initial selection rounds were subjected to more MD simulations, up to 80×10 ps and 5×100 ps.
Nevertheless, computational library design for enzyme engineering has serious challenges, of which reliability of predictions is an important example.
The results confirmed the working hypothesis that obtaining unique binding orientations, reflected in high enantioselectivity, was easier with the bulkier substrates.
a larger fraction of the stilbene oxide designs displayed high NAC frequencies and high predicted enantioselectivities than what was found with cyclopentene and butene oxide designs.
10 of the 20 inspected variants for stilbene oxide displayed structural problems.
Chiral analysis of the formed diols revealed that 21 (77%) of the designed active variants showed the enantioselectivity predicted by MD simulations (10% ee cutoff, Table 2).
the main cause of the lower catalytic activities of most designs found during initial tests (Table 2) is due to a higher $K_{M}$ , not a lower $k_{cat}$

The observation that $k_{cat}$ values are lowered in the designs for 3a whereas NAC percentages during MD simulations (Table 2) were fine indicates that prediction of reactivities across different substrates using such short MD simulations is troublesome.

The high enantioselectivities obtained for stilbene oxide variants, relative to the designs for the two other substrates, are likely due a more restricted conformational freedom in case of the bulky stilbene oxide.
the 3-fold lower $k_{cat}$ of mutant 60A with 3a was accompanied by a 6-fold better $K_{M}$ , leading to an improved catalytic efficiency in 60A.
The best conditions (10% dioxane and 40°C) were combined and gave a conversion of 86 and 63% for variants 60A and 41B, respectively.
Increasing the dioxane concentration to 15% was beneficial for the proRR variant 60A, yielding a conversion of 98%
the nucleophilic water molecule stays in virtually the same position
The enantioselectivity of the enzyme is therefore determined by the positioning of substrate relative to this water.

the two most $(S,S)$ -diol selective mutants for 3a (52A, 41B) carried mutation I116F, a mutation that introduces steric hindrance at the central side and in case of substrates 1a and 2a favors $(R,R)$ -selectivity
The unexpected $(S,S)$ -diol preference might be due to $\pi-\pi$ interactions between the substrate and the newly introduced aromatic ring of Phe116.
there is good overall agreement only in qualitative terms, that is, $(R,R)$ - or $(S,S)$ -diol stereopreference was correctly predicted for 77% of the variants

experimental activities for individual variants did not correlate with their computed NAC%, showing that the MD simulations as performed here do not provide quantitative information on catalytic rates.
This is not unexpected, as MD does not account for energy barriers along reaction coordinates.
the best enantioselectivities were clearly obtained with stilbene oxide 3a
four of the observed enantioselectivities with stilbene oxide 3a were opposite to the Rosetta design target
Two of these were corrected in MD simulations
computational design and MD simulations are well able to predict and screen enantioselectivity of LEH variants in case of small aliphatic substrates
Whereas highly selective variants for production of aromatic diols can be obtained, prediction accuracy is lower

Reaction mixtures (total volume 1 mL) contained 10 mg of cis-stilbene oxide (final concentration 50 mm, suspension) and 6.37 mg of enzyme (final concentration 320 μm) in 50 mm HEPES, pH 8.0, and were incubated (at 30 or 40°C, 135 rpm) for 48 h.

For the other strongly $(R,R)$ -selective mutant, 38A with ee >99%, the introduction of steric hindrance at the proSS side (F134W) can explain the improvement in enantioselectivity along the same lines as for substrates 1a and 2a.

Surprisingly, the two most $(S,S)$ -diol selective mutants for 3a (52A, 41B) carried mutation I116F, a mutation that introduces steric hindrance at the central side and in case of substrates 1a and 2a favors $(R,R)$ -selectivity (see above).

Also, for one of the two most proRR selective variants (60A, ee >99%) the I80W mutation would be expected to decrease space at the peripheral side and thereby stimulate proSS selectivity.

The same selectivity by attraction might hold for one of the best $(R,R)$ -selective mutants: variant 60A (ee >99%) carries the I80W mutation at the peripheral (proRR) side and no steric hindrance introducing mutation on the proSS site.

For both variants that are strongly proSS selective for 3a the only bulk introducing mutation is I116F, which would be expected to introduce steric hindrance at the central side of the cavity and thus stimulate proRR selectivity.
Indeed, designs for substrate 1a and 2a carrying both L35W and I116V gave an (S,S)-diol preference of >73% ee (Table 2).

In case of cyclopentene oxide 1a, directed evolution $^{[47,50-52]}$ and previous computational design $^{[32]}$ gave $(S,S)$ -selective LEH variants producing the diol with similar ee as found here (60–95% ee, Table 2).
The conversion of cis-stilbene oxide by variant 60A was further improved (63 to 80%) by increasing the reaction temperature from 30 to 40°C.

$^{[60]}$ In silico screening methods have been used earlier to design cocaine hydrolyzing enzymes with improved catalytic efficiency, $^{[61,62]}$ but also to design libraries of a cytochrome P450 harboring variants with controlled selectivity, $^{[63]}$ and to increase amidase activity in an esterase.
Increasing the dioxane concentration to 15% was beneficial for the proRR variant 60A, yielding a conversion of 98%, but drastically decreased conversion of 3a by the proSS variant 41B.
Addition of 10% dioxane gave the best conversion, yielding up to 78% diol in 44 h at 30°C.

$^{[50-52]}$ To examine if these redesigned LEHs could be used in preparative scale conversions, the conversion of cis-stilbene oxide 3a to the $(R,R)$ - and $(S,S)$ -diols by variants 60A and 41B was examined under different reaction conditions, including varying temperatures and cosolvents (Table 4).

$^{[57-59]}$ The LEH template used here (LEH-P, $T_{m,app}=70^{\circ}C$ , PDB 4R9K) is not the most stable enzyme from our previous work, as it lacks the disulfide bonds of the most stable variant ( $T_{m,app}=85^{\circ}C$ , PDB 4R9L).
I116, F134 and F139) were selected to mutate simultaneously to any of the nine hydrophobic residues (AFGILMPVW).
Whereas most designs produced $(R,R)$ -diol 3b (with ee >99% for two variants), enantiocomplementary mutants yielding $(S,S)$ -3b were also found, with a highest ee of 97%.
This might be due to an increase in the volume of the substrate binding cavity, which grew by $37 \AA^{3}$ in mutant 46C that had a 76-fold increase in $K_{M}$ for 1a.
Variants 43A and 24A had lower catalytic constants (2.5- and 7-fold, respectively).

When variants with a predicted increase of the active site volume of $>100\ \AA^{3}$ would have been removed from the libraries, only the primary Rosetta libraries for cis-stilbene oxide would have shrunken (elimination of 178 of the 442 variants listed in Table 1).

This included two variants (51A, 60A) that were originally designed using Rosetta to have $(S,S)$ -3b selectivity but for which the HTMI-MD screening predicted preferential formation of $(R,R)$ -diol, which was in agreement with what was observed experimentally.

The potential of this computational approach was illustrated for limonene epoxide hydrolase (LEH) redesign in a previous study $^{[32]}$ where we observed that the performance of the best enzymes in a 37-variant library obtained by the CASCO framework was similar to that of the best variants obtained by screening approximately 4700 variants generated by the CAST-ing strategy for directed evolution.
The purity of the prepared enzymes (yield 50–150 mg per L TB medium) was analyzed by SDS-PAGE.
For each designed new variant 20–80 independently started MD simulations of 10 ps were used (previously maximally 20 MD simulations).
(with ee 99%) but only modest (S,S)-selective variants (ee 44%).

$^{[47,50,52]}$ Such well-optimized directed evolution protocols still rely on substantial experimental screening by chiral chromatography, which triggered us to examine if computational methods could be developed to enrich libraries and replace most of the laboratory screening by computational screening of protein libraries.
Remarkably, adding THF as co-solvent (similar properties as 1,4-dioxane) gave lower conversion than reaction conditions without cosolvent or with 10% dioxane.
The LEH variants 41B and 60A were active in this suspension.
The stilbene oxide enantioselectivities of designs 41B and 52A are higher than reported for other LEH variants tested on this substrate.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Computational design of enantiocomplementary epoxide hydrolases for asymmetric synthesis of aliphatic and aromatic diols from meso-epoxides, aiming to reduce experimental screening while achieving high enantioselectivity.

canonical_events.summary: Directed evolution for enantioselective enzymes requires extensive high-throughput screening, which is time-consuming and expensive.
Computational design using the CASCO framework (Rosetta + MD) can reduce library size and predict enantioselectivity, enabling larger jumps in sequence space.
Design targeted 11 active-site positions randomized to 9 hydrophobic residues for proRR or proSS attack, generating thousands of Rosetta designs per substrate.
MD screening of 15,000 Rosetta designs with at least 5×10 ps simulations; top designs received up to 80×10 ps and 5×100 ps. NAC% scored on-the-fly.
Stilbene oxide designs had higher NAC frequencies and predicted enantioselectivities than smaller substrates (7.1% vs 1.5% and 3.8%).
Visual inspection revealed structural problems in 10 of 20 inspected stilbene oxide designs, indicating Rosetta design failures.
28 new LEH variants constructed and tested; 26 active. 77% of active variants showed predicted enantioselectivity (10% ee cutoff).

For cyclopentene oxide (1a): 9/10 active, all predicted correctly; proSS designs gave higher ee (56–85%) than proRR. For butene oxide (2a): 10/10 active, 8/10 predicted correctly; proSS better (best 77% ee).

For stilbene oxide (3a): 6/8 active predicted correctly; two mismatches, including variant 52A predicted (R,R) but gave (S,S) with 97% ee. Four enantioselectivities opposite to Rosetta design target; MD corrected two.
Best enantioselectivities obtained with stilbene oxide: >99% ee for (R,R)-diol (variant 60A) and 97% ee for (S,S)-diol (variant 52A).
Preparative scale conversions of stilbene oxide using variants 60A and 41B: 60A gave 98% conversion, >99% ee (R,R); 41B gave 63% conversion, 88% ee (S,S). Dioxane best cosolvent.
Kinetic analysis: lower activities of most designs due to higher KM, not lower kcat. For stilbene oxide, kcat lower but KM better, indicating MD does not predict reactivities across substrates.

Structural origin of enantioselectivity: substrate positioning relative to fixed nucleophilic water determines selectivity. For small substrates, steric hindrance steers selectivity. For stilbene oxide, π-π interactions may play a role (e.g., I116F giving unexpected (S,S)-selectivity).
MD-based NAC scoring provides qualitative but not quantitative prediction of enantioselectivity; no correlation between NAC% and activity. MD does not account for energy barriers.
Computational design and MD simulations predict enantioselectivity well for small aliphatic substrates but less accurately for aromatic substrates. Need improved scoring for π-π interactions.

edges.reason: The problem of extensive screening motivates the hypothesis that computational design can reduce library size.
The hypothesis leads to the design strategy targeting active-site positions.
The designed variants are subjected to MD screening.
MD screening results show higher NAC frequencies for stilbene oxide.
Despite high NAC frequencies, visual inspection reveals structural problems in stilbene oxide designs.
Top designs from MD screening are experimentally tested.
Experimental results for small substrates are analyzed.
Experimental results for stilbene oxide reveal prediction failures.
Despite failures, high enantioselectivities are achieved for stilbene oxide.
Best variants are tested at preparative scale.
Kinetic analysis explains activity changes in designs.
Structural analysis explains enantioselectivity for small substrates.
Structural analysis explains unexpected selectivity for stilbene oxide.
Overall prediction accuracy is assessed, revealing limitations.
Limitations lead to the conclusion about prediction accuracy and need for improved scoring.

must_cover_points: Problem of extensive screening in directed evolution
Hypothesis of computational design reducing library size
Design strategy targeting 11 positions with 9 hydrophobic residues
MD screening of 15,000 designs with NAC scoring
Experimental testing of 28 variants with 77% prediction accuracy
Prediction failures for stilbene oxide (e.g., variant 52A)
High enantioselectivities achieved for stilbene oxide (>99% ee)
Limitation: qualitative but not quantitative prediction; no correlation between NAC% and activity
Conclusion: good prediction for aliphatic substrates, lower accuracy for aromatic substrates; need improved scoring for π-π interactions
